chunks.text: The artist Van Gogh could barely sell any of his paintings during his lifetime.
He was rejected and criticized by ordinary people and was pushed to mental breakdown.
Just before he took his own life at thirty - seven
he had earned only the price of a single painting.
Without the support from his brother Theo, he could not even have bought paint.
Despite all this, Van Gogh created about 2,100 works in ten years.
Paintings and drawings, an enormous number together.
His dedication was not in pursuit of success.
Decades after his death, his paintings began to be reevaluated.
Today
" Sunflowers " and " The Starry Night " are counted among the most popular paintings in the world.
They are traded for billions of yen each.
While Van Gogh was alive, no one could perceive his potential.
The exception was only his brother Theo.
Theo believed in his brother's talent and, at considerable sacrifice, kept supporting him.
Van Gogh's story asks about the definition of success.
Is success to be recognized in life?
Is success to leave recognition after death?
Perhaps, for Van Gogh himself, success was " to keep painting.
" Whatever the reaction of the world, he kept painting according to his inner voice.
That was the integrity of his life.
We can see the truth of a human being inside the canvases Van Gogh left behind.
Colors that seem to yell, a life force that seems to burst.
Instead of staying silent, he continues to speak to the world through his paintings.
